column5: Blockchain can secure verification and ownership transfer history to restore trust in land governance in Tamale.
column7: Orientation and preliminary learning, Team alignment
column8: Landtify strengthens land governance at the community level by enabling chiefs and their secretaries to issue and verify land allocations through a secure, transparent digital system and also allow land-buyers to verify land ownership before making payments for parcels of land they want to acquire. At the regional level, it reduces land disputes, restores trust in traditional authorities, and supports safer housing and investment across Northern Ghana. At the national level, Landtify creates a foundation for future integration with the Lands Commission, improving land documentation and formal registration. At a continental level, it demonstrates how blockchain can be applied to Africa’s traditional governance systems in a regenerative, community-led way.
column9:
Today, land governance in many communities in Northern Ghana relies on manual, paper-based records managed by traditional authorities. While culturally legitimate, these systems are isolated, difficult to verify, and vulnerable to duplication and disputes.
The immediate potential for change is the introduction of a simple digital verification layer that mirrors existing workflows, allowing chiefs and their secretaries to issue and store land records digitally, enabling land buyers and investors to securely verify land parcels without altering traditional authority structures.
Over time, this can evolve into a shared, community-level registry where land parcels have traceable ownership histories, reducing disputes and strengthening trust. In a later stage, these verified community records can support smoother engagement with formal institutions such as the Lands Commission, enabling easier registration, planning, and investment.
The end-state vision is not full automation or replacement, but a higher-order land governance system where tradition, transparency, and technology work together to support peace, security, and sustainable development.
column10:
At the start of the research, we understood land disputes in Northern Ghana mainly as a governance failure or lack of regulation. Through research, stakeholder discussions, and validation, our perception shifted significantly. We realized that traditional land systems are not the problem, they are legitimate, respected, and functional, but are constrained by manual, fragmented record-keeping that cannot scale or guarantee verification.
A key surprise was the openness of chiefs, secretaries, surveyors, and buyers to transparency and technology. Rather than resisting change, they expressed frustration with the limitations of current tools and welcomed solutions that strengthen, not replace, traditional authority. This insight unlocked a new direction for our work: designing a system that works with existing structures rather than attempting to formalize or bypass them.
What initially called our team into action was the recurring pattern of land disputes, financial loss, and community tension. What emerged through the research was a clearer opportunity, to regenerate trust, reduce conflict, and support sustainable development by bridging tradition and technology in a practical, respectful way.
column11:
We’re keeping things simple and flexible as we work on Landtify.
Roles:
I.Muhsin Ismail – Facilitator & Technical Lead: leading the tech side, especially the blockchain and prototype.
II.Tahiru Abdul Gafaru – Design & Documentation: making the interface look good and keeping track of what we’re building.
III.Abdul Kadir Insuaf, Alhassan Takeeya Mandeiya, Hardi Zakiya – Design & Documentation: helping with UI, visuals, and notes.
IV.Abdul Rahman Mardia, Hardi Zakiya – UX / Research: talking to chiefs, secretaries, and buyers to understand the real problems.
How we work together:
I.Quick weekly check-ins to see what everyone’s doing and what’s next.
II.Tasks tracked casually on Trello/Notion so we know who’s doing what.
III.Research feeds into design, which feeds into tech, but we constantly check in with each other to make sure things make sense.
IV.We test ideas as we go and adjust based on feedback from each other and stakeholders.
Resources:
I.Laptops, internet, and shared online tools to coordinate.
II.Cardano SDKs and blockchain docs for building the prototype.
III.Access to chiefs, secretaries, and buyers for early feedback and validation.
This setup keeps us flexible, collaborative, and focused on building something that actually works for the communities we’re trying to help.

column3: Goma Hub — Goma, North Kivu, DR Congo
column5: Digital commerce works, but without trust and transparency it limits local growth, seller credibility, safe adoption, and youth inclusion.
column8: Wenze plays a structuring role at multiple regional levels, starting in the city of Goma and extending to the wider regional and blockchain ecosystems. At the local level, it formalizes informal digital commerce already happening on WhatsApp and social media by providing a secure, transparent marketplace where buyers, sellers, promoters, and service providers can transact safely using escrow and reputation mechanisms. At the city and provincial level, Wenze strengthens youth-led economic activity by transforming smartphones into verified digital shops, making transactions traceable and enabling future access to microcredit, entrepreneurship programs, and economic support. At the ecosystem level, Wenze serves as a practical gateway to the Cardano blockchain, translating everyday mobile money payments into blockchain-recorded transactions, thus driving real-world adoption of decentralized technology in an inclusive way. Overall, the project connects local informal economies in Goma to regional digital and blockchain systems, fostering trust, transparency, and sustainable economic participation.
column9:
In our current context in Goma, digital commerce already exists but operates in a fragmented and fragile state. Transactions mainly occur through private messages on social networks, without records, guarantees, or mechanisms to build trust. This limits growth, excludes many honest sellers, and keeps economic activity invisible and unrecognized. The system today functions at a survival level: people trade because they must, not because the system supports them.

The potential for change lies in gradually structuring what already exists rather than replacing it. In the first stage, Wenze introduces basic safeguards such as escrow, transaction records, and reputation, allowing users to trade with greater confidence without changing their habits or requiring technical knowledge. In a second stage, repeated use creates visible histories of transactions and trust, enabling sellers and service providers to build credible digital identities and buyers to make informed decisions. At a more advanced stage, this structured data can support higher-order capabilities, such as access to micro-finance, youth entrepreneurship programs, and locally relevant blockchain use cases, while remaining grounded in everyday needs. The end state is not a fully automated or complex system, but a simple, trusted digital market where local economic activity is recognized, measurable, and able to grow sustainably.
column10:
At the start of the programme, our understanding of the local context was largely based on observation and personal experience: frequent online scams, lack of trust in digital transactions, and limited practical use of blockchain technologies in everyday life. Our initial purpose was driven by a desire to address these risks and demonstrate that blockchain could be relevant to real economic needs in Goma.

As the programme progressed, conducting a field survey became a turning point. The survey helped us move beyond assumptions and better understand the actual behaviors, frustrations, and expectations of potential users. It confirmed the scale of informal digital commerce, highlighted how common fraud and failed transactions are, and revealed a strong demand for simple tools that provide security, proof, and accountability without adding complexity. This process reshaped our perception of the system: we realized that the issue was not resistance to innovation, but the absence of trust-building mechanisms. The key shift was understanding that meaningful impact would come from organizing and legitimizing existing practices, not replacing them, which clarified our purpose and strengthened our confidence in the project’s potential.
column11:
We are organised as a small, cross-functional team formed during a structured hackathon process, with clear roles, simple coordination tools, and iterative workflows.

Roles: Responsibilities are distributed based on skills and availability. Developers focus on the mobile app, backend, and Cardano integration; researchers and analysts handle problem definition, user research, and survey analysis; all members contribute to testing, documentation, and feedback. This ensures that even non-technical members add tangible value.

Resources & tools. We use WhatsApp for daily coordination, GitHub for code collaboration and task management, shared documents for notes and decisions, and regular online and in-person meetings to align progress. Surveys are used as a core resource to ground decisions in real user needs.

Processes: We work iteratively: define a problem, validate it through discussion and surveys, implement a feature, test it as a team, and document outcomes. Technical work progresses in small milestones (login, wallet connection, escrow foundations), while research and feedback run in parallel.

Structure: The team operates with lightweight governance: regular check-ins, clear action items after each meeting, and shared documentation to ensure transparency and continuity. This structure allows us to move fast, adapt to constraints, and steadily build toward the project’s potential without over-engineering our organisation.

column5: Low-income people lack reliable mobility and depend on overcrowded, and inflexible bus systems. An affordable and flexible alternative is needed.
column8:
At the local level, particularly within university campuses and surrounding neighborhoods, the project provides students and low-income populations with affordable and reliable access to electric mobility. By reducing dependence on overcrowded buses, it improves daily commuting, punctuality, and personal autonomy.
At the city level, the project contributes to easing pressure on public transportation systems by offering a complementary shared mobility option. It supports cleaner urban transport, reduces congestion, and encourages the adoption of environmentally sustainable practices through electric bicycles.
At the national level, the project serves as a scalable model for inclusive mobility that can be replicated across multiple cities facing similar transportation challenges. It also promotes digital financial inclusion by introducing transparent, blockchain-based payment systems accessible to underbanked populations.
At the regional (African) level, the project aligns with broader goals of sustainable urban development and green mobility. By leveraging the Cardano blockchain, it demonstrates how decentralized technologies can be applied to real-world social challenges, fostering trust, accountability, and cross-border innovation within the African tech ecosystem.
column9:
Today, urban mobility in many cities is limited and unreliable. Public buses are often overcrowded, unavailable during off-peak hours, and inflexible, leaving students and low-income communities with few options. Existing alternatives, such as taxis or informal rides, are expensive, inconsistent, and lack transparency.
We envision a gradual transformation toward a more inclusive, reliable, and sustainable mobility ecosystem. In the short term, deploying shared electric bicycles at key local hubs can immediately improve accessibility, reduce pressure on buses, and offer a flexible transport option for students and daily commuters. Digital payments and simple tracking systems can provide transparency, even for users without traditional banking access.
As adoption grows, the system can scale to integrate more stations, optimize routes based on usage data, and implement smart contracts on Cardano to automate payments, deposits, and usage rules. Over time, this can evolve into a city-wide, community-managed network that balances demand and supply dynamically, encourages sustainable transport, and creates a replicable model for other cities.
The end-state vision is a trusted, decentralized, and scalable shared mobility system that empowers communities, reduces environmental impact, and demonstrates the potential of blockchain to solve real-world social challenges without relying solely on centralized authorities.
column10:
At the start of the programme, our focus was primarily on the visible challenge: overcrowded buses and limited mobility options for students and low-income communities. This initial observation motivated our team to explore a solution that could provide a more reliable and flexible alternative.
As the programme progressed, our perception of the local context deepened significantly. Through discussions, mentorship, and on-the-ground research, we discovered the complexity and interdependence of urban mobility systems: peak-hour crowding, gaps in coverage, informal transport practices, and social barriers to access. We were surprised by how pervasive the reliance on unreliable transport was and how much it constrained daily life, productivity, and social participation.
These insights shifted our focus from simply providing more vehicles to creating a holistic, decentralized mobility ecosystem. We realized that by integrating blockchain technology, we could not only facilitate secure payments and trust but also empower communities to manage and optimize mobility collectively. The programme revealed that meaningful change comes from addressing both infrastructure gaps and social trust, unlocking a more ambitious yet realistic vision for sustainable urban mobility.
column11:

- The community manager who handles outreach and community investigation.

column9: Axiom can transform Kenya’s healthcare ecosystem by decentralizing payments, automating insurance claims, and giving patients verifiable control over their medical data. Today, systems are fragmented, opaque, and vulnerable to breaches. By leveraging Cardano, Axiom enables secure, auditable transactions while maintaining privacy. Phased adoption:starting with clinics and insurers, can scale regionally, improving efficiency, trust, and compliance. This approach creates a sustainable, transparent, and patient-centric digital healthcare system that can serve as a model for East Africa and later Africa.
column10: Over the course of the programme, our perception of Kenya’s healthcare and digital finance landscape has shifted significantly. Initially, our focus was on creating a secure digital payment platform, but we discovered deeper challenges around patient data privacy, fragmented insurance workflows, and limited patient agency. The key insight was that decentralization could not only streamline payments but also empower patients and strengthen trust across the ecosystem. This realization broadened our purpose: Axiom now aims to integrate privacy-first data management, transparent insurance verification, and scalable blockchain solutions, creating measurable social and systemic impact across local and regional healthcare networks.
column11: Our team is organizing around clear roles and processes to realize Axiom’s potential. I lead product vision and Cardano integration, while other team members focus on frontend, backend, and UI/UX development. We operate in sprints, using collaborative tools for project management and version control. Currently, we lack access to the Andamio platform, which limits some blockchain development resources, and we are seeking a Cardano-experienced developer to strengthen smart contract implementation and optimize on-chain/off-chain integration. Our structure emphasizes iterative prototyping, secure data management, and phased adoption to ensure a working, scalable solution for Kenya’s healthcare ecosystem.

At the local level, it enables smallholder farmers to verify products, access fair markets, and receive payments securely. At the national level, it supports agricultural cooperatives and exporters with traceable, blockchain-backed trade records that reduce fraud and inefficiencies. Regionally, the platform lays the foundation for interoperable, cross-border agricultural trade by providing shared standards for identity, product provenance, and settlement, supporting more inclusive and resilient regional supply chains.
column9: Today, agricultural trade for smallholder farmers is fragmented, opaque, and dependent on intermediaries that limit trust and access to global markets. In the near term, FarmerConnect enables digitally verifiable identities, product records, and escrow-based transactions to reduce fraud and payment delays. As adoption grows, these trusted records can interconnect farmers, cooperatives, and buyers into a shared trade layer. Over time, this supports a more transparent, efficient regional agricultural market where farmers build reputational history and participate confidently in cross-border trade.
column10: At the start, we viewed the problem mainly as a technical challenge of enabling cross-border payments and market access for farmers. Through the programme, we realized the deeper issue is trust: verification of products, identities, and transactions is what truly limits participation. A key shift was understanding that small, verifiable records can unlock larger market confidence. This insight reframed our purpose from building a marketplace to creating a trust layer that empowers farmers and reduces dependency on intermediaries.
column11: We are organised as a cross-functional team with clearly defined roles across product leadership, frontend, backend, and smart contract development. We leverage Cardano tools for smart contracts and on-chain verification, alongside shared repositories and documentation for transparency. column8:
This project helps people in communities earn rewards for cleaning up their neighborhood. It uses a simple phone app to take pictures of their work. Other people in the community then check the pictures to make sure the work is real. Once it's checked, the information is saved forever on the Cardano blockchain and the person who did the cleaning gets a reward.

It gives local groups and governments a clear way to see what work is being done and where help is needed most. The system is built to be copied in other cities across Kenya and East Africa, showing how technology can help solve local problems.
column9:
Today, the system for community cleanups in places like Kibera is mostly informal and invisible. A group might clean an area, but there is no trusted record of what was done. Donors and local governments don't know who to support, and volunteers get tired because they aren't recognized or rewarded. Any recycling that happens is scattered and hard to track.

Our project introduces the first simple change: making impact visible and verifiable. By using a basic app and community checks, we create a trusted, tamper-proof record of who cleaned what and when. This is the crucial first step.

In the near term, this visibility creates new potential. When impact is proven, small rewards can flow directly to the people doing the work, making their effort sustainable. Local recyclers can see a steady, verified supply of materials, and small sponsors can confidently support specific, proven projects.

The natural end-state we see is a self-sustaining local ecosystem. The reward system is funded partly by the sale of recycled materials and local sponsorship, not just outside donors. Cleaner neighborhoods become a source of local pride and small income. The system runs on the trust it creates and the small value it unlocks from waste, becoming a permanent, community-owned tool for improvement. The change is from invisible, unpaid work to visible, valued, and sustainable community action.
column10:
Initially, our team saw the problem in Kibera as a straightforward lack of waste management infrastructure. We thought the solution was simply to organize more cleanups and connect them to recyclers. Our purpose was to build a bridge between a visible problem and an available solution.

However, working directly with the community through this programme revealed a deeper, more human layer. The biggest surprise was not the lack of action, but the lack of trusted proof. We met many dedicated individuals and youth groups who were already cleaning regularly, but their work was invisible to anyone outside their immediate area. This rendered them ineligible for support and drained their motivation.

This was the key shift in our perception: The primary bottleneck isn't activity, but verifiable credibility.

This insight fundamentally changed our project. We stopped focusing just on logistics (collecting more waste) and started focusing on trust infrastructure. We realized that by solving the verification problem first—using simple tools the community already has, like mobile phones—we could unlock everything else. Verified work can attract micro-rewards. Verified plastic supply builds stronger partnerships with recyclers. Verified impact data attracts local business sponsorships.

Our purpose evolved. We are no longer just building a cleanup service. We are building a system of recognition and economic enablement for the work that is already happening. The potential we see now is far greater: a context where community environmental stewardship is not a hidden sacrifice, but a visible, valued, and viable contribution to the local economy.
column11:
Our team is organizing around key local partnerships to ensure the project is grounded and effective.

Core Team:
- Project Lead & Community Liaison: SHIELD International lead, based in Nairobi.
- Technical Lead: Developer specializing in Flutter and Cardano integration.
- Verification Designer: Focuses on user experience for logging and community validation.

Community Pilot Team:

- Seed Validator Group: 15-20 initial community members testing the verification process.

Key Partners:
- Gjenge Makers: Plastic recycling company that will purchase verified, sorted plastic collected through the system.
- Local Youth Groups: Already active in cleanup efforts in Kibera, who will be early adopters.
- Mtaa Safi Initiative: Community-based organization focused on local sanitation, providing grassroots network and trust.

Resources & Process:
We work in weekly development sprints followed by immediate testing with the Kibera team. Bi-weekly community reviews guide our priorities. A dedicated ADA reward treasury will fund the pilot incentives. Communication is managed through shared documents and regular check-ins, keeping the structure lean and focused on rapid learning from the community.

column5: Kenya's gig workers can't prove skills or guarantee payment. 40% youth unemployment exists alongside desperate demand. Missing: trust infrastructure.
column8:
TrustGig's Regional Role: Building East Africa's Trust Economy

Local Level: Community Economic Activation

Within Nairobi neighborhoods and Kenyan towns, TrustGig acts as **economic connective tissue**—linking the plumber in Kibera with the client in Westlands, the electrician in Nakuru with the business in town. We transform informal, high-risk transactions into structured, protected exchanges that keep money circulating locally while building individual prosperity.

City Level: Urban Labor Market Infrastructure

In Nairobi and Kenya's growing cities, TrustGig becomes **critical infrastructure for the informal economy**—the 83% of Kenyan employment that operates outside formal systems. We provide what banks, courts, and traditional institutions don't: instant reputation verification, automated payment protection, and dispute resolution accessible to people earning $2-10 per gig. We're the trust layer the informal economy desperately needs.

National Level: Youth Employment Pipeline

Across Kenya, TrustGig addresses the **40% youth unemployment crisis** not through job creation but through market correction—enabling the skilled workforce that already exists to actually sell their services. We transform blockchain-verified training programs into economic opportunity, creating pathways from unemployment to gig work to premium service provider. We're building the bridge between Kenya's education system and economic participation.

Regional Level: East African Skills Marketplace

As the platform scales, TrustGig becomes **East Africa's portable reputation system**. A Kenyan driver with excellent ratings can work in Uganda or Tanzania with credentials that cross borders. Regional labor mobility increases without sacrificing worker protection or client confidence. We're creating the infrastructure for an integrated East African gig economy where skills and reputation travel with workers.

Continental Level: African Tech Innovation Model

TrustGig demonstrates **blockchain solving African problems, built by Africans**—not imported solutions adapted poorly. Our focus on physical services, low-bandwidth design, mobile-first interface, and community mediation reflects uniquely African contexts. Success here provides blueprints for Lagos, Accra, Kigali, and beyond, proving blockchain's utility beyond speculation.

Global Level: Future of Work Infrastructure

In the broader context, TrustGig pioneers **decentralized trust for the informal economy**—a model relevant to the 2 billion people worldwide in informal work. We're proving that blockchain can protect vulnerable workers, enable reputation portability, and create fair market access without requiring formal institutions, traditional banking, or centralized platforms extracting excessive fees.

The Through-Line

At every level, TrustGig plays the same essential role: **we're trust infrastructure that enables economic activity currently paralyzed by coordination failure.** Locally, we connect neighbors. Nationally, we activate youth potential. Regionally, we enable labor mobility. Globally, we demonstrate technology serving human dignity.
column9:
TrustGig's Evolution: From Coordination Tool to Economic Infrastructure

Where We Are Today (Current State)

Kenya's gig economy operates on **pure personal networks and cash transactions**. A young plumber finds work through family connections, WhatsApp groups, and word-of-mouth. Payment happens in cash after work—if it happens at all. There's no way to verify if someone claiming plumbing skills actually has them. No protection if a client refuses payment. No record of the 50 excellent jobs completed last year. Each new client relationship starts from absolute zero trust.

This system works barely, inefficiently, and unfairly. Talented workers stay unemployed because they lack the right connections. Clients overpay for mediocre work because they can't identify quality. Both parties absorb high risk with every transaction.

Phase 1: Basic Coordination (Months 1-6)

Goal: Prove the core value proposition works

We start simple: workers create profiles, clients post jobs, basic escrow holds payment until work completion. Just these fundamentals represent massive improvement—workers get paid reliably, clients see basic rating histories, disputes have simple resolution paths.

Success looks like: 200 active workers, 1,000 completed gigs, 85% transaction completion rate, measurable reduction in payment disputes. We're not transforming economies yet; we're proving people will use blockchain-protected transactions for everyday services.

Phase 2: Reputation Accumulation (Months 6-18)

Goal: Demonstrate that on-chain reputation translates to economic gains

Workers accumulate verified transaction histories. Early adopters who've completed 50+ gigs can now demonstrate track records to new clients. We introduce credential verification—training certificates recorded on-chain. Workers with verified electrical training command 20-30% premium rates over unverified competitors.

Success looks like: Measurable wage increases for high-reputation workers, training institutions integrating blockchain credentials, workers actively managing their on-chain profiles as career assets. The system proves reputation portability creates real economic value.

Phase 3: Network Effects (Months 18-36)

Goal: Reach critical mass where the platform becomes indispensable

Enough workers and clients participate that TrustGig becomes the default way to find skilled services in major Kenyan cities. Clients preferentially hire through the platform because verified workers are more reliable. Workers join because that's where clients are. The flywheel accelerates.

Training programs integrate directly—graduates receive blockchain credentials automatically. Community organizations use our mediation tools. Insurance products emerge around highly-rated workers. The platform becomes infrastructure, not just an app.

Success looks like: 10,000+ active workers, dominance in 3+ service categories, partnerships with 20+ training institutions, self-sustaining growth from word-of-mouth.

Phase 4: Skill Mobility (Years 3-5)

Goal: Enable economic progression through verifiable skill development

Workers use the platform not just for gigs but for career advancement. A basic cleaner completes housekeeping certification courses, records credentials on-chain, qualifies for hotel contracts. A driver adds defensive driving and customer service certificates, becomes eligible for corporate accounts. An electrician progresses from outlet repairs to solar installation through documented apprenticeships.

The platform maps skill progression pathways. Workers see: "Complete these three certifications to qualify for jobs paying 2x your current rate." Training becomes investment with clear ROI.

Success looks like: Measurable income progression curves, 40%+ of workers completing additional training, documented cases of workers moving from survival gigs to sustainable livelihoods.

Phase 5: Regional Integration (Years 5-7)

Goal: Create East Africa's first portable reputation system

A Kenyan driver with excellent TrustGig reputation moves to Kampala and immediately accesses work—credentials follow seamlessly. Regional labor mobility increases without sacrificing protection. Cross-border remittances flow through the platform. Training credentials earned in Tanzania are recognized in Kenya.

We're not creating a new currency or replacing governments—we're providing a layer of trust that enables existing economic activity to happen more efficiently across borders.

Success looks like: 15%+ of workers operating cross-border, regional training standard adoption, measurable increase in labor mobility without increase in fraud.

 End-State Vision (7-10 Years)

An economy where trust is infrastructure, not a luxury

TrustGig becomes what roads are to transportation—invisible infrastructure enabling economic activity. Young Kenyans entering the workforce expect portable, blockchain-verified credentials. Clients hiring workers check on-chain reputations as automatically as checking phone numbers. Training programs default to blockchain credentialing. Disputes resolve through established mediation without courts or lawyers.

The platform has succeeded when it feels unremarkable—when blockchain-protected transactions are just how gig work happens, and the alternative (cash transactions with strangers, no verification, no protection) seems as outdated as bartering.

What Success Actually Looks Like

We're not eliminating unemployment—formal job creation remains essential. We're not replacing banks or governments—traditional institutions still matter. We're not making everyone wealthy—we're creating fairer market access.

But we are measurably: reducing payment disputes by 80%+, enabling 30-50% income increases for skilled workers through reputation effects, cutting time-to-first-gig for new workers from months to days, and creating pathways from unemployment to sustainable self-employment for thousands of young Kenyans.
column10:
How Nairobi Blockchain Meetups Reshaped Our Understanding

Where We Started: Technology Looking for Problems

Initially, the Nairobi blockchain community gathered around fascination with the technology itself. We were excited about decentralization, smart contracts, and cryptocurrency—the tools were compelling, and we assumed applications would follow naturally. The implicit assumption was: "Blockchain is revolutionary, so let's find ways to apply it to Kenya."

We thought we were looking for problems that blockchain could solve. We were actually looking for excuses to use blockchain.

First Shift: From Technology Push to Problem Pull

The unlock came from listening to actual community pain points rather than pitching blockchain solutions. When Fiona Makena described youth unemployment—not as an abstract statistic but as her cousin with electrical training waiting tables, her neighbor with plumbing skills driving a boda-boda—the problem became visceral and specific.

The surprise was realizing Kenya doesn't need blockchain; Kenya needs trust. Blockchain is just one tool for building it.

This shifted our entire orientation. Instead of asking "What can blockchain do in Kenya?", we started asking "What stops talented Kenyans from earning fair livings, and could blockchain help?" The technology moved from protagonist to supporting actor.

Second Shift: Discovering the Invisible Infrastructure Gap

Through meetup discussions, we recognized something initially invisible: Kenya has sophisticated informal systems that work remarkably well, but they hit hard limits around trust between strangers.

Within tight communities—family, neighbors, church groups—economic activity flows smoothly through social capital and reputation. But the moment transactions cross community boundaries, everything breaks down. A plumber known and trusted in Kibera can't leverage that reputation in Westlands. Credentials earned in one context become worthless in another.

The insight: We weren't facing a lack of markets or skills. We were facing a coordination failure caused by non-portable reputation. Blockchain suddenly made sense not as revolutionary technology, but as mundane infrastructure—a way to make reputation portable across community boundaries.

Third Shift: Safety as Economic Infrastructure, Not Social Issue

Initially, we thought about worker safety as a "nice to have" feature—important ethically, but secondary to core economic functions. Then during one meetup, Empress shared her friend's experience: a young woman turned down better-paying cleaning jobs because she couldn't verify client safety.

This reframed everything. Safety features weren't add-ons; they were **economic enablers**. Location sharing, client verification, and behavior ratings weren't about being socially responsible—they were about unlocking economic participation for half the workforce.

Young women represent massive untapped skilled labor, held back not by capability but by legitimate safety concerns. Solving safety unlocks economic potential. This wasn't obvious initially; it emerged from actual worker voices in meetup conversations.

Fourth Shift: Dignity Over Disruption

The tech world celebrates "disruption"—breaking existing systems, moving fast, replacing the old. But Kenyan meetup participants consistently emphasized **dignity and respect for existing informal systems**.

The surprise: People didn't want their livelihoods disrupted. They wanted them protected and enhanced. Workers weren't looking for handouts or charity technology—they were looking for tools that let them compete fairly in markets that currently disadvantage them through information asymmetry and trust deficits.

This shifted the entire product philosophy. We weren't building a disruptive platform to replace informal networks. We were building infrastructure that makes informal networks work better—that lets reputation earned through real work translate to economic opportunity.

As Empress said: "We're not just building apps; we're building trust infrastructure." That single framing represented months of perspective evolution.

Fifth Shift: From Individual Gigs to Economic Progression

Early discussions focused narrowly on single transactions—get hired, do work, get paid, repeat. But workers kept bringing up progression: "How do I go from basic work to premium work? How do I prove I'm getting better?"

The insight: A gig platform that doesn't enable upward mobility just creates a different form of economic trap. Workers need more than fair payment for current skills; they need visible pathways to develop better skills that command higher rates.

This fundamentally changed the product vision. We weren't building a transaction platform; we were building economic mobility infrastructure. Blockchain-verified training credentials weren't features—they were the bridge between where workers are and where they could be.

Sixth Shift: Understanding What "Regional" Actually Means

Initially, we thought "regional potential" meant expanding to other African countries—standard startup scaling logic. Discussions with workers who'd moved between Kenya, Uganda, and Tanzania revealed something different.

Regional potential meant letting workers take their reputations with them. A driver who built excellent ratings in Nairobi but moved home to rural Tanzania shouldn't start from zero. A Ugandan electrician working temporarily in Kenya shouldn't be disadvantaged against local workers with established reputations.

The unlock: Regional isn't about company expansion; it's about worker mobility without economic penalty. This completely reframed what "scale" means for TrustGig.

What Called Us to Action (And How That Changed)

Initial purpose: Build something cool with blockchain technology, maybe help people along the way.

Current purpose: Create infrastructure that enables skilled Kenyans to earn dignified livings by making trust portable, transactions secure, and reputation verifiable—because the talent exists, the market exists, and only coordination failure keeps them apart.

Local Context Contains Solutions

Perhaps the biggest shift was moving from "How do we apply global blockchain innovations to Kenya?"to"What do Kenyan communities already know about trust and reputation that blockchain can encode?"

Kenya's informal economy already has sophisticated reputation systems—they just don't scale beyond immediate communities. Village elders already mediate disputes—they just can't do it for digital transactions. Training programs already certify skills—those certifications just aren't portable or verifiable.

The potential isn't in replacing these systems with blockchain. The potential is in amplifying what already works using technology that makes it scale.

That perspective shift—from bringing solutions to communities, to encoding community wisdom in infrastructure—represents the fundamental evolution of our understanding. We're not imposing technology. We're building tools that let existing social capital work at internet scale.

Team Organization

Core Team Roles

Leonard Boma - Product Lead: Platform design, user research with gig workers, feature prioritization based on community feedback

Development Team - Smart contract development (escrow, reputation systems), mobile-first frontend (low-bandwidth optimized), backend infrastructure

Empress - Community & Operations: Worker onboarding, training institution partnerships, mediation protocol design

Business/Strategy - Pricing models, sustainability planning, regulatory navigation

Key Resources

- Nairobi blockchain meetup network for testing and feedback
- Partnerships with training institutions for credential integration
- Community mediators for dispute resolution
- Mobile money infrastructure (M-Pesa integration)
- Worker co-op models from existing informal networks

Process

Build-Test-Learn cycles: Ship minimal features to small worker groups, iterate based on real transaction data. Start with 20-50 workers in one service category (plumbing), prove the model, expand.

Community-led development: Monthly meetups for user feedback, workers help design features they'll actually use

Phased rollout: Single city (Nairobi) → Multiple cities → Regional expansion, only scaling what's proven

Structure

Lightweight team, heavy community involvement. Workers aren't just users—they're co-designers. Platform governance eventually includes worker representatives in key decisions.

Focus: Ship something small that works, then grow based on demonstrated value, not projected potential.

column8:
1. Farm-Level System (Micro-Region):
Acts as a digital cooperative & bank for individual smallholders and family farms. It transforms their physical harvest into a bankable, tradable digital asset on their own phones & digital devices, enabling access to credit and premium markets previously restricted by geography and scale.

2. County-Level System (Pilot Regions - e.g., Kericho, Kisii):
Serves as the integrating platform & trust layer for the county's agricultural ecosystem. It connects disparate actors—farmers, cooperatives, local processors, county government certification officers—onto a single, immutable ledger. This creates a "Digital Organic Denomination of Origin" for the county's specialty crops (e.g., Kericho Organic Tea, Kisii Avocado).

3. National Kenyan System:
Positioned as a critical national infrastructure for agricultural transparency and finance. It bridges the trust gap between Kenya's productive agricultural regions and its major urban consumption centers (Nairobi, Mombasa) as well as export gateways. By standardizing organic and provenance data on-chain, it elevates "Made in Kenya" agricultural brand integrity for the global market.

4. East African / Continental System (Future):
Aims to become a protocol for cross-border agricultural trade and climate finance. The system designed for Kenyan counties can be replicated, creating interoperable regional food corridors. It positions Cardano as the settlement and verification layer for premium, traceable African produce entering international supply chains and carbon markets.
column9:
Current State (Today's System)
The agricultural value chain in our pilot regions is fragmented and analog. Trust is personal, not systemic. Transactions are cash-based, records are paper or memory, and verification is expensive and slow. A farmer's crop is an illiquid physical good until the moment it's sold to the next link in a long, opaque chain.

Evolutionary States of Change
State 1: The Digital Twin (18-24 months)

Change: The physical harvest gets a verifiable digital identity on Cardano (a token with rich metadata: farm location, organic practices, harvest date).

Capability Leap: For the first time, the asset (the crop) can be separated from its physical location. It can be financially referenced, pre-sold, or used as collateral before it reaches the market. The system moves from tracking transactions to defining assets.

State 2: The Integrated Trust Layer (3-4 years)

Change: This digital asset becomes the common language connecting all actors. The cooperative, the processor, the exporter, and the certification body all interact with and add to the same on-chain record.

Capability Leap: The entire local supply chain operates on a single source of truth. Disputes vanish, compliance is automated, and the cost of trust plummets. The system evolves from managing assets to orchestrating stakeholder workflows.

State 3: The Regional Market Engine (5+ years)

Change: These tokenized assets from multiple counties flow into a liquid, regional market. Price discovery becomes transparent. DeFi protocols allow for yield-bearing deposits of crop tokens, pre-harvest financing, and seamless cross-border settlement.

Capability Leap: The agricultural economy transitions from a series of isolated, seasonal cash sales to a continuously traded, data-rich value market. Capital can flow efficiently to where it's needed most, based on verifiable on-chain performance.

End-State Vision (A Sustainable Ecosystem)
We see a self-sustaining agricultural data economy in the region. The premium for "verified sustainable Kenyan organic produce" is locked in by global, immutable proof. Farmers are no longer just producers; they are validators and beneficiaries of their own data, earning from their crops and their climate-positive practices. The system's capability is no longer just economic efficiency, but resilience and value distribution, attracting talent and innovation back to rural areas. The ledger is maintained not by us, but by the value it creates for every participant in the chain.
column10:
Initial Calling: The Technical Solution
Our team first came together driven by a technologist's conviction. We saw Cardano's potential—its sustainability, native assets, and smart contracts—as the perfect technical solution to the well-documented problems of agricultural opacity and financial exclusion in Kenya. Our initial perception was of a system in need of a superior tool. We aimed to build that tool: a blockchain-based ledger to bring transparency to supply chains.

Key Shifts and Unlocked Insights
1. From "Supply Chain" to "Value Web"

Surprise: The most profound shift was moving beyond the linear "farm-to-fork" model. Through conversations with farmers and cooperatives, we realized the system isn't a chain but a complex web of relationships, informal credit, shared assets, and social trust.

New Insight: Our platform couldn't just track a product; it had to reinforce and digitize these existing social and economic webs. This led to our core design principle: the cooperative, not the individual farm, as the primary onboarding node. The technology must serve social cohesion, not disrupt it.

2. From "Organic as a Label" to "Organic as a Data Journey"

Surprise: We initially saw organic certification as a binary badge—a problem of fraud to be solved with verification. We learned it's a continuous practice, documented through seasonal labor, input purchases, and soil management.

New Insight: This unlocked the concept of rich, time-stamped metadata attached to a crop token. The value isn't just in the "organic" claim at the end, but in the verifiable story of stewardship along the way. This story is what justifies premiums and secures loans.

3. From "Banking the Unbanked" to "Assetizing the Invisible"

Surprise: The financial barrier wasn't just a lack of banks. It was that a farmer's primary asset—a growing crop—is invisible and illiquid to any formal system.

New Insight: Our pivotal moment was realizing we weren't building a bridge to traditional finance. We were building a parallel system to recognize and activate dormant value. A token isn't just a receipt; it's a capital asset the moment the seed is sown. This changed everything, making DeFi not an add-on, but the core economic engine.

4. From "Export Focus" to "Heritage Value"

Surprise: While export markets are lucrative, the deepest enthusiasm from farmer groups was for indigenous vegetables (terere, mrenda, saget). These crops are tied to cultural identity, nutritional security, and climate resilience, but are marginalized in commercial markets.

New Insight: We saw our platform's potential to create a new market architecture that values cultural and nutritional capital as highly as export dollars. This isn't just about efficiency; it's about curation and celebration of heritage, using technology to preserve and scale traditional knowledge.

Evolved Perception of Potential
Initially, we saw potential in fixing a broken system. Now, we see potential in growing a new one.

The local context is not a deficit of trust, but an abundance of social capital waiting for a complementary digital layer. It is not a lack of assets, but a world of unrecognized latent value in soils, seeds, and sustainable practices.

Our purpose has evolved from implementing a blockchain solution to orchestrating a new form of inclusive, data-driven agricultural commons. The potential we now see is for Kenyan farming communities to leapfrog not just technologies, but entire stages of financial and market development, using Cardano to build a more equitable, transparent, and resilient food system from the ground up.
column11:
Organizational Structure: Hub & Spoke with Technical Pods
We operate as a lean, mission-driven core team in Nairobi with distributed field operations, organized to maximize efficiency and local impact.

Core Team Roles & Responsibilities
1. Technical Development Pod (Nairobi Hub)

Emmanuel Odero (CTO): Technical architecture, Cardano protocol integration, smart contract security oversight

Peter Maina (Blockchain Developer & Project Manager):

Lead Aiken smart contract development

DeFi protocol design and implementation

Project timeline management and milestone tracking

Technical documentation and audit coordination

Kalondu Muema (UI/UX Developer & Client Satisfaction Officer):

Mobile-first PWA development (React/Next.js, MeshJS integration)

User experience design for low-literacy farmers

Kiswahili/English interface localization

Farmer feedback collection and iteration management

2. Field Operations Network (County Spokes)

9 County Coordinators (one per pilot county):

Hired locally from agricultural communities

Fluent in local languages and farming practices

Responsible for farmer onboarding, training, and support

Act as the primary liaison between farmers and tech team

Cooperative Integration Specialists:

Embedded within partner cooperatives

Facilitate group onboarding and collective bargaining

Resource Allocation & Budget Implementation
Development ($200,000 - 40%):

$80,000 Aiken Contracts: 2 developers (including Peter) for 12 months

$50,000 MeshJS Integration: Kalondu's focus + 1 frontend contractor

$30,000 Blockfrost API: Infrastructure and data pipeline setup

$40,000 Mobile App: Development and testing across 100+ device types

Farmer Onboarding ($150,000 - 30%):

$60,000 County Training: $6,500-7,000 per county for workshops, materials, demonstrations

$50,000 Coordinator Salaries: Stipends for 9 coordinators + 2 supervisors

$25,000 Equipment: Basic smartphones, QR code printers, charging stations

$15,000 Localization: Translation, voice interfaces, cultural adaptation

Partnerships & Marketing ($100,000 - 20%):

$40,000 Cooperative Partnerships: MOUs, integration support, incentive programs

$25,000 Digital Marketing: Targeted campaigns in pilot counties

$20,000 Conference Participation: Nairobi AgriTech Expo, County Agricultural Shows

$15,000 Content Creation: Farmer success stories, tutorial videos in Kiswahili

Operations ($50,000 - 10%):

$25,000 Legal & Compliance: Company registration, data privacy, agricultural regulations

$15,000 Infrastructure: Cloud hosting, SMS gateway, API services

$10,000 Administrative: Office space, communication tools, accounting

Key Processes & Workflows
1. Agile Development Cycle (2-week sprints):

Sprint planning based on crop cycle calendars (e.g., tea harvest, maize planting)

Daily standups for tech team, weekly sync with field coordinators

Feature prioritization driven by farmer feedback tickets from county coordinators

2. Farmer-Centric Design Process:

Co-creation Sessions: Regular design workshops with farmers in each county

"Phone-Shed" Testing: All features tested on low-end Android devices in field conditions

Voice-First Validation: Every interface validated for voice navigation before deployment

3. Field-to-Code Feedback Loop:

Weekly "Mazao" Reports: Each coordinator submits farmer challenges and successes ("mazao" means crops/harvest)

Bi-weekly Live Demos: Tech team demonstrates new features to coordinators via video call

Monthly Impact Metrics: Track farmer income changes, loan uptake, transaction volumes per county

4. Cooperative Integration Framework:

Phase 1: Map existing cooperative processes (collection, payment, record-keeping)

Phase 2: Digitize 1-2 key processes using HarvestLedger

Phase 3: Train cooperative leadership on platform management

Phase 4: Transition to cooperative-led onboarding and support

Governance & Decision-Making
Technical Decisions: Led by Emmanuel (CTO) with input from development team
Product Decisions: Led by Kalondu (UX/Client Satisfaction) informed by farmer feedback
Field Operations: Managed by Peter (Project Manager) with county coordinators
Strategic Decisions: Consensus among core team based on impact metrics and budget considerations

Escalation Path: Farmer → County Coordinator → Client Satisfaction Officer → Core Team Review

Risk Mitigation Structure
Technical Risks:

Weekly code reviews and peer programming for smart contracts

Gradual rollout: Testnet → Small pilot → County-wide → Multi-county

Adoption Risks:

Incentive-first approach: Start with immediate value (market access, premium pricing)

Social proof: Focus on respected farmers and cooperative leaders as early adopters

Fallback systems: Maintain parallel paper records during transition period

Operational Risks:

County redundancy: No single point of failure; multiple counties in similar agro-ecological zones

Knowledge sharing: Cross-training between coordinators from different counties

Contingency budget: 10% of each category held for unexpected challenges

Success Measurement Framework
Weekly:

Farmer sign-ups per county

Transaction volume and frequency

App engagement metrics

Monthly:

Income change surveys (sample of farmers)

Loan repayment rates

Platform revenue vs projections

Quarterly:

Farmer retention rates

Cooperative partnership renewals

Technical performance (uptime, transaction speed)

Communication Structure
Internal:

Daily: Tech team standup (Slack/Teams)

Weekly: All-hands sync (coordinator reports + demo)

Monthly: Strategy review (metrics + budget)

External:

Bi-weekly: Project Catalyst community updates

Monthly: Farmer newsletter (SMS/WhatsApp in Kiswahili)

Quarterly: Public progress report (blog + video)

This organizational structure ensures we remain agile enough to iterate based on field learnings while maintaining rigorous enough processes to deliver a secure, scalable platform. The distribution of responsibilities leverages each team member's strengths while creating clear accountability for results.

column8:
Community / Village Level (Primary Impact Layer)

Role:
VCB functions as a digital trust and savings infrastructure for village banking groups, cooperatives, and informal savings circles.

Problems addressed:

Manual record-keeping and cash-based systems

Weak identity verification and leadership abuse

Loss of savings through fraud or mismanagement

Exclusion from formal banking due to distance and cost

VCB’s role:

Digitizes village savings and loan records

Introduces transparent, tamper-resistant ledgers

Enables group-based financial governance

Preserves community autonomy while improving accountability

Outcome:
Stronger financial discipline, trust, and continuity within rural communities.
Regional Level (SADC / Sub-Saharan Africa)

Role:
VCB operates as a replicable community-finance model adaptable across similar socio-economic environments.

Why it applies regionally:

Shared reliance on informal savings groups

Similar barriers to banking access

Common trust and governance challenges

Growing mobile penetration with limited financial depth

VCB’s role:

Provides a modular digital banking framework

Encourages regional standards for community finance transparency

Supports cross-border learning and interoperability

Acts as a proof-of-concept for ethical fintech in rural Africa

Outcome:
A scalable, culturally aligned community-first fintech blueprint.
column9:
Where the System Is Today

Community savings and lending groups already function across the region, but at a very basic capability level:

Records are handwritten and easy to lose or manipulate

Group rules depend heavily on personalities and trust

Leadership changes often disrupt continuity

Disputes are difficult to resolve objectively

External support actors struggle to verify legitimacy

These systems work, but they are fragile.
column10:
How Our Perception of the Local Context and Its Potential Has Changed

At the beginning of the programme, our team was primarily motivated by a clear and practical problem: village banking groups were losing money and trust due to poor record-keeping, weak transparency, and limited access to secure financial tools. Our initial assumption was that the main challenge was technological — that introducing a digital or blockchain-based system would largely solve these issues.

Over the course of ideation and research, our understanding shifted significantly.

Key Shift: From Technology to Governance

One of the most important insights was that the core problem is not the absence of technology, but the absence of reliable governance structures at the community level. Many groups already operate effectively with minimal resources, but they lack systems that preserve institutional memory, enforce agreed rules consistently, and protect members during leadership transitions.

This realization reframed our approach. Rather than focusing on advanced features, we began prioritizing simplicity, clarity, and rule support.

Surprises That Changed Our Thinking

Resilience of informal systems
We were surprised by how well village banking groups function despite very limited tools. This highlighted the need to support, not replace, existing practices.

Sensitivity around trust and control
We learned that communities are cautious about external systems that appear to take control away from them. This reinforced the importance of community ownership and transparency over automation.

Low tolerance for complexity
Even small increases in process complexity can discourage adoption. This insight led us to reduce our scope and focus on essential functions only.
column11:
VillageChain Bank is organized as a small, multidisciplinary core team with clearly defined responsibilities. The structure is intentionally lean to allow fast learning, accountability, and efficient coordination.

Roles and Responsibilities
1. Product & Design Lead (Team Lead)

Role focus: Vision, usability, and coherence

Responsibilities:

Overall product direction and scope control

UX/UI design, prototyping, and user flow definition

Translating community needs into simple digital features

Coordinating between technical and non-technical team members

Value to the project:
Ensures the system remains accessible, culturally appropriate, and aligned with real user behavior.

2. Software Engineering Lead

Role focus: Platform logic and application development

Responsibilities:

Backend and application-layer development

Data models for savings, loans, and group governance

API design and system integration planning

Supporting MVP implementation and iteration

Value to the project:
Turns concepts into a working, maintainable system while keeping complexity under control.

3. Networking & Information Security Lead

Role focus: Infrastructure reliability and data protection

Responsibilities:

Network architecture and deployment planning

Security, identity, and access-control considerations

Risk assessment for data integrity and availability

Advising on hosting, scaling, and resilience

Value to the project:
Protects user data and system trust, which is critical in financial contexts.

4. Community & Market Engagement Lead

Role focus: Adoption, communication, and feedback

Responsibilities:

Community engagement and stakeholder communication

User onboarding and feedback collection

Market positioning and messaging

Adoption strategy and trust-building

Value to the project:
Ensures the system is understandable, accepted, and responsive to user needs.

Working Processes
Incremental Development

Start with a minimal, usable MVP

Validate with real users before adding features

Prioritize stability and clarity over speed

Feedback-Driven Iteration

Regular community feedback sessions

Adjust features based on observed usage, not assumptions

Keep documentation lightweight and practical

Clear Decision Ownership

Each role has defined decision authority

Major scope changes are discussed collectively

Avoids decision paralysis and role overlap

Resources and Tools

Design: Low-fidelity wireframes, clickable prototypes

Development: Version control, issue tracking, modular architecture

Documentation: Shared technical and user documentation

Communication: Regular check-ins and clear action items

Resources are chosen for accessibility and sustainability, not sophistication.

Governance and Accountability

Shared project roadmap and milestones

Transparent task ownership

Regular progress reviews

Clear definition of “done” for each phase

This ensures the project remains focused and accountable without introducing heavy bureaucracy.

column5: Unregulated mining in Tolon degrades land and water due to weak monitoring, poor data transparency, and limited community involvement.
column7: Orientation and preliminary learning, Team alignment
column8:
TerraGuard plays a critical role in strengthening mining governance and environmental protection in the Northern Region of Ghana, particularly in the Tolon community. At the community level, it empowers residents and traditional authorities to report and track mining activities using a transparent, tamper-proof digital system, giving them an active role in safeguarding land and water resources.
At the district and regional level, TerraGuard provides the District Assembly, Environmental Protection Agency (EPA), and other regulators with real-time, reliable data on mining permits, site activities, and violations. This improves coordination, enforcement, and evidence-based decision-making.
At the national and systemic level, the project demonstrates how blockchain technology can modernize natural resource monitoring, reduce corruption, and support sustainable mining practices. TerraGuard therefore acts as a bridge between communities, regulators, and policy frameworks, helping align local environmental protection efforts with broader regional and national sustainability goals.
column9:
Currently, mining oversight in the Tolon community is largely reactive, fragmented, and dependent on manual inspections and informal reporting. Data is scattered, enforcement is slow, and communities have limited influence over decision-making despite being directly affected.
In the near term, the system can evolve by introducing simple digital reporting and transparent record-keeping through TerraGuard. This enables basic visibility of mining activities, clearer identification of illegal sites, and improved communication between communities and district authorities.
In the medium term, as stakeholders gain trust in the system, coordinated monitoring becomes possible. Regulators begin to rely on shared, verified data for inspections and enforcement, while communities transition from passive observers to recognized co-monitors.
In the end-state vision, Tolon achieves a stable, accountable mining oversight system where illegal activities are deterred through transparency rather than force, environmental harm is reduced, and decisions are guided by reliable data. This represents a practical shift to a higher level of governance capability without requiring complex infrastructure or unrealistic institutional change.
column10:
At the start of the programme, our team viewed the mining challenges in the Tolon community mainly as an enforcement and compliance problem caused by weak regulation and limited resources. Our initial motivation was to find a technical solution that could help authorities monitor mining activities more effectively.
As the programme progressed, our perception shifted significantly. We were surprised to discover that the core challenge was not just the absence of rules, but the lack of trustworthy, shared information and meaningful community participation. Communities possessed valuable knowledge about mining activities, yet their insights were rarely captured or acted upon.
This realization unlocked a new insight: the greatest potential for change lies in connecting community knowledge with transparent systems, rather than relying solely on top-down enforcement. TerraGuard emerged from this shift in thinking moving from a purely technical tool to a participatory monitoring system that recognizes communities as key actors in sustainable mining governance.
column11:
Our team is organized around three clear and complementary roles to effectively develop TerraGuard, currently at the user research and solution proposal stage.
Technical Lead: Responsible for defining the technical feasibility of the solution, exploring appropriate blockchain architectures, and outlining system requirements based on the identified problem and context.
User Research and Community Liaison: Leads engagement with the Tolon community, traditional authorities, and relevant stakeholders to understand current mining practices, challenges, and user needs. This role ensures local realities and community knowledge shape the proposed solution.
UX and Design Lead: Focuses on mapping user journeys, identifying key interaction points, and translating research insights into clear, user-centered solution concepts and proposals.
At this stage, the team works collaboratively to gather insights, align on the problem definition, and develop a well-grounded solution proposal before moving into design and implementation.
